mnemonics.title: Heavy Metal Hazards
mnemonics.content: LPMCH - Lead (brain/kidney), Mercury (nervous), Cadmium (bones/kidney), Chromium (cancer), Hexavalent Chromium causes DNA mutation

acronyms.usage: Discarded electrical and electronic devices that have reached end-of-life
Toxic synthetic chemicals used in transformers and capacitors, now banned but persist in old equipment
EU directive regulating collection and recycling of e-waste
Policy making manufacturers responsible for end-of-life product management
Chemicals added to plastics to reduce flammability, persistent in environment
Older display technology containing significant lead content
Rechargeable battery type containing toxic cadmium

visualTips.visualization: Create a body diagram - draw head for Lead (brain), kidneys for Cadmium, nervous system for Mercury, bones for Chromium. Color-code each organ with the metal color (lead=gray, mercury=silver, cadmium=orange, chromium=blue)
Draw an inverted pyramid - Top (widest): Reduce/Reuse, Middle: Recycle (primary/secondary), Bottom (narrowest): Dispose. Shows preference for top layers
Draw a food chain pyramid - Bottom: small organisms (absorb toxins), Middle: small fish (eat organisms), Top: large fish/eagle (highest concentration). Show toxin arrows increasing in size going up

analogies.analogy: Like a snowball rolling downhill that keeps growing - as technology advances, the e-waste snowball grows faster and larger each year
Like a bank account that only deposits but never withdraws - once heavy metals enter the body, they accumulate over a lifetime
Like trying to extract gold from a mine by hand without any safety gear - dangerous, inefficient, and exposes workers to toxic hazards
Like filling a bathtub with the drain plugged - water keeps coming in (toxin exposure) but nothing goes out, so the tub overflows (toxic buildup)
Like an uninvited guest who never leaves and brings friends - POPs persist in environment, accumulate, and travel long distances through food chain
